title: Cauda Equina Syndrome
oneLiner: Acute spinal nerve root compression causing bilateral sciatica, saddle anaesthesia, and bladder/bowel dysfunction; a neurosurgical emergency.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Spinal cord compression (e.g. metastatic cord compression, transverse myelitis):** presence of a sensory level on the trunk (dermatomal line) – this suggests cord, not cauda equina lesion, and requires high-dose dexamethasone and urgent MRI.
- **Spinal epidural abscess / haematoma:** fever + severe back pain + rapid neurological decline (within hours) especially in an anticoagulated or septic patient – needs emergency MRI and drainage; delaying antibiotics or reversing anticoagulation is catastrophic.
- **Aortic dissection / rupture:** sudden tearing back/abdominal pain + hypotension + asymmetrical leg pulses – immediate CT aorta and vascular surgery; this mimics radicular pain but is never "sciatica".
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Bilateral or severe unilateral sciatica (often asymmetrical) with low back pain
  - Saddle anaesthesia – loss of sensation in the perineum, buttocks, posterior thighs (S2–S5 dermatomes)
  - Bladder dysfunction: urinary retention (most sensitive sign), incontinence, poor stream
  - Bowel dysfunction: faecal incontinence or severe constipation
  - Lower limb weakness (often distal, e.g. foot drop, reduced plantarflexion)
  - Recent onset of sexual dysfunction (rarely volunteered)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Loss of perianal sensation (test with pinprick and light touch in all four quadrants)
  - Reduced anal sphincter tone on PR exam (ask patient to squeeze your finger)
  - Post-void residual volume >100 mL (ideally measured by bladder scanner – if >200 mL, high suspicion)
  - Lower limb reflexes: reduced or absent knee and ankle jerks (LMN pattern)
  - Plantar responses: flexor (unless coexisting cord lesion)
  - Straight leg raise may be positive but not diagnostic
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely -> less likely:**
  - **Lumbar disc herniation (without CES)** – unilateral sciatica, no saddle anaesthesia, normal bladder function, normal perianal sensation
  - **Spinal stenosis (neurogenic claudication)** – bilateral leg pain on walking, relieved by sitting/forward flexion, no acute bladder symptoms, no saddle loss
  - **Conus medullaris syndrome** – earlier bowel/bladder involvement, often painless, plus upper motor neuron signs (upgoing plantars, hyperreflexia) and sensory level on trunk
  - **Spinal cord compression (metastasis, haematoma, abscess)** – sensory level on trunk, upper motor neuron signs (spasticity, Babinski) below lesion, may have fever (abscess) or known malignancy
  - **Peripheral neuropathy (e.g. Guillain-Barré)** – symmetrical ascending weakness, absent reflexes, but no saddle anaesthesia, no bladder retention (early), often preceded by infection
- **Splitter:** **Sensory level on the trunk** = spinal cord lesion (above conus) vs **perianal/saddle loss without sensory level** = cauda equina lesion. Also: **lower motor neuron signs (areflexia, flaccid weakness)** in cauda equina vs **upper motor neuron signs (hyperreflexia, Babinski, spasticity)** in spinal cord.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Bladder scanner for post-void residual (PVR) – if unable to void or PVR >100 mL, suspect CES
  - Digital rectal exam (perineal sensation and anal tone)
  - Full neurological exam including sensory mapping of perineum (use dermatome chart)
  - Urine dipstick (exclude UTI as cause of retention)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, coagulation screen (PT/APTT), U+E, glucose, inflammatory markers (if abscess suspected – blood cultures)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Urgent MRI of the whole spine (including lumbar and thoracolumbar junction) – ideally within 1–4 hours of clinical suspicion
  - **Second-line:** CT myelogram if MRI contraindicated (e.g. non-MRI compatible pacemaker, severe claustrophobia) – but less sensitive for nerve root compression
- **Special tests:** None routine; if infection suspected – lumbar puncture (only after MRI to rule out abscess) – [discuss with senior]
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **Clinical diagnosis – no formal scoring system.** Use NICE red flags: bilateral sciatica, saddle anaesthesia, bladder retention, progressive weakness, loss of perianal sensation + reduced anal tone.
  - **Canadian C-spine rule or other** not applicable here.
- **Never-miss test:** **Urgent MRI whole spine** – omission is the single most serious error; delayed diagnosis >48 hours from symptom onset dramatically worsens prognosis for bladder/bowel recovery.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Spinal precautions** – log roll if moving patient, but no collar unless trauma; avoid flexion/rotation if vertebral instability suspected
  - **Urgent neurosurgical referral** – ideally within 1 hour of suspicion
  - **MRI as soon as possible** – do not wait for blood results
  - **Catheterisation** – if urinary retention (post-void residual >200 mL), insert a urethral catheter to decompress bladder and monitor output
  - **Analgesia** – paracetamol 1 g IV/PO, ibuprofen 400 mg PO if no contraindication (avoid NSAIDs if anticoagulated or surgical decompression imminent), add IV morphine 2.5–5 mg PRN for severe pain
  - **Do NOT give steroids** – high-dose dexamethasone is indicated only for spinal cord compression (not cauda equina); it may delay surgery and increase infection risk
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Admit under **Neurosurgery** (or Orthopaedics with neurosurgical support)
  - **Neurological observations** – hourly limb power, perianal sensation, bladder volume/catheter output
  - **Bladder chart** – strict input/output, aim for catheter removal only after neurosurgery advice
  - **Bowel care** – if faecal incontinence, consider bowel regimen (e.g. suppositories, enemas) after neurosurgery clearance
  - **VTE prophylaxis** – discuss with neurosurgery; usually low molecular weight heparin (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily) can be started **after** surgical site is haemostatic and only if no bleeding risk (e.g. spinal haematoma). In acute CES, defer until after surgical plan is clear.
  - **Surgery** – decompressive laminectomy and discectomy (or abscess drainage) ideally within 24–48 hours of symptom onset; earlier is better for neurological recovery
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Spinal rehabilitation** – physiotherapy, occupational therapy, pelvic floor physiotherapy
  - **Bladder training** – if neurogenic bladder: intermittent self-catheterisation, urology referral
  - **Bowel programme** – stimulant laxatives (senna) + suppositories, dietary fibre, bowel retraining
  - **Chronic pain management** – neuropathic agents (gabapentin/pregabalin – start low, e.g. gabapentin 300 mg nocte, titrate up) – only after acute phase
  - **Sexual health counselling** – if relevant
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride at 125 mL/h if NBM; otherwise oral free fluids
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** [check local policy] – usually enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily, but **hold** until neurosurgery confirms no spinal haematoma risk (often post-op day 1 if haemostasis adequate)
  - **Diet:** NBM if surgery planned <6 hours; otherwise normal diet
  - **Observation frequency:** Neuro obs every 4 hours (limb power, perianal sensation, bladder status)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Analgesia:**
    - Paracetamol: 1 g PO/IV QDS (max 4 g/day)
    - Ibuprofen: 400 mg PO TDS (if no contraindication: GFR >30, no bleeding risk, not anticoagulated, not post-surgery)
    - Morphine: 5–10 mg IM/IV PRN severe pain (min 4-hourly; monitor respiratory rate)
  - **Laxatives (if chronic constipation):** Senna 15 mg PO nocte + lactulose 15 mL PO BD (after acute phase)
  - **Neuropathic pain (if chronic):** Gabapentin 300 mg PO nocte for 1 day, then 300 mg BD, then 300 mg TDS – max 3600 mg/day; or Pregabalin 75 mg PO BD, max 600 mg/day – [check local policy for titration]
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Amitriptyline 10–25 mg PO nocte for neuropathic pain (if no cardiac history, caution in elderly)
  - Oxycodone if morphine not tolerated: start 5 mg PO PRN
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid in renal impairment (GFR <30), GI bleeding, coagulopathy, and in immediate pre‑/post-operative spinal surgery (increased bleeding risk)
  - Gabapentin/pregabalin: reduce dose in renal impairment (GFR <60) – [check local policy]
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Gabapentin: reduce dose if CrCl <60 mL/min (e.g. 300 mg every other day if GFR 30–60); pregabalin: reduce similarly
  - NSAIDs: avoid if eGFR <30
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **None specific to CES** – but **withholding anticoagulation in a patient with epidural haematoma could be catastrophic**, while giving enoxaparin pre-operatively can cause spinal haemorrhage. Always verify timing with neurosurgery.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Permanent paraplegia (rare, usually due to delayed treatment or alternative diagnosis like cord infarction)
  - Urinary retention → renal injury if not catheterised
  - Sepsis if secondary UTI
  - Spinal epidural haematoma post-surgery (if anticoagulated prematurely)
- **Chronic:**
  - Irreversible bladder dysfunction (neurogenic bladder, faecal incontinence) – occurs in ~20–30% even with timely surgery
  - Chronic neuropathic pain (saddle area, perineum, legs)
  - Sexual dysfunction (erectile dysfunction, loss of vaginal sensation)
  - Lower limb weakness (persistent foot drop) – may require ankle-foot orthosis
  - Psychological distress (depression, adjustment disorder)
sections.Prognosis:
- **Mortality:** Low (<1% from CES itself unless from underlying cause like abscess/sepsis).
- **Recovery:** Best if surgical decompression within 48 hours of symptom onset – up to 80% recover bladder function; if >48 hours, permanent bowel/bladder dysfunction is common. Motor recovery (leg strength) can occur over weeks to months but may be incomplete.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **I**n any patient with acute back pain + leg symptoms, **always ask about bladder function and perianal sensation** – this non-intrusive question can catch CES early.
- **Perform a bladder scan** (post-void residual) at the bedside – a PVR >100 mL (or >200 mL in some guidelines) is the single most objective finding and often prompts timely MRI.
- **If you suspect CES, call neurosurgery immediately** – do not wait for MRI; they can organise and prioritise imaging while you prepare the patient.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT** send a patient with bilateral sciatica to physiotherapy or prescribe simple analgesia and discharge without assessing perianal sensation and bladder function.
- **Do NOT** rely solely on a normal digital rectal exam (DRE) – early CES can have normal anal tone; absence of perianal sensation is more sensitive.
- **Do NOT** delay MRI in favour of a CT spine (lumbar) – CT misses disc fragments and early nerve root compression; MRI is mandatory.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** (rarely, if MRI negative and symptoms mild)
  - Any new or worsening leg weakness (e.g. foot drop, inability to stand from chair)
  - Loss of bladder control (inability to pass urine or leaking)
  - New numbness or tingling in the saddle area (between legs, around anus)
  - Severe or progressive back pain not controlled by simple analgesia
- **Follow-up:** **Neurosurgery or Orthopaedic spine clinic** within 2–4 weeks; **community physiotherapy**; **GP review** in 1 week for medication and neurological status.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / neurosurgery registrar immediately if:**
  - Sudden deterioration in limb power (e.g. dropping from MRC 4 to 2 over 1 hour)
  - New onset of urinary retention (inability to void, PVR >200 mL)
  - Loss of perianal sensation previously present
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 (unlikely from CES alone but consider sepsis from UTI/dehydration) – treat as medical emergency
  - Lactate > 2 mmol/L (if measured – may indicate ischaemia or sepsis)
- **Call crash team / ICU if:**
  - GCS < 13 (rare but consider meningitis/spinal shock) or cardiorespiratory compromise from underlying cause (e.g. aortic dissection, myelopathy)

rawMarkdown:
## Cauda Equina Syndrome

**One-liner:** Acute spinal nerve root compression causing bilateral sciatica, saddle anaesthesia, and bladder/bowel dysfunction; a neurosurgical emergency.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Spinal cord compression (e.g. metastatic cord compression, transverse myelitis):** presence of a sensory level on the trunk (dermatomal line) – this suggests cord, not cauda equina lesion, and requires high-dose dexamethasone and urgent MRI.
- **Spinal epidural abscess / haematoma:** fever + severe back pain + rapid neurological decline (within hours) especially in an anticoagulated or septic patient – needs emergency MRI and drainage; delaying antibiotics or reversing anticoagulation is catastrophic.
- **Aortic dissection / rupture:** sudden tearing back/abdominal pain + hypotension + asymmetrical leg pulses – immediate CT aorta and vascular surgery; this mimics radicular pain but is never "sciatica".

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Bilateral or severe unilateral sciatica (often asymmetrical) with low back pain
  - Saddle anaesthesia – loss of sensation in the perineum, buttocks, posterior thighs (S2–S5 dermatomes)
  - Bladder dysfunction: urinary retention (most sensitive sign), incontinence, poor stream
  - Bowel dysfunction: faecal incontinence or severe constipation
  - Lower limb weakness (often distal, e.g. foot drop, reduced plantarflexion)
  - Recent onset of sexual dysfunction (rarely volunteered)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Loss of perianal sensation (test with pinprick and light touch in all four quadrants)
  - Reduced anal sphincter tone on PR exam (ask patient to squeeze your finger)
  - Post-void residual volume >100 mL (ideally measured by bladder scanner – if >200 mL, high suspicion)
  - Lower limb reflexes: reduced or absent knee and ankle jerks (LMN pattern)
  - Plantar responses: flexor (unless coexisting cord lesion)
  - Straight leg raise may be positive but not diagnostic

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely -> less likely:**
  - **Lumbar disc herniation (without CES)** – unilateral sciatica, no saddle anaesthesia, normal bladder function, normal perianal sensation
  - **Spinal stenosis (neurogenic claudication)** – bilateral leg pain on walking, relieved by sitting/forward flexion, no acute bladder symptoms, no saddle loss
  - **Conus medullaris syndrome** – earlier bowel/bladder involvement, often painless, plus upper motor neuron signs (upgoing plantars, hyperreflexia) and sensory level on trunk
  - **Spinal cord compression (metastasis, haematoma, abscess)** – sensory level on trunk, upper motor neuron signs (spasticity, Babinski) below lesion, may have fever (abscess) or known malignancy
  - **Peripheral neuropathy (e.g. Guillain-Barré)** – symmetrical ascending weakness, absent reflexes, but no saddle anaesthesia, no bladder retention (early), often preceded by infection
- **Splitter:** **Sensory level on the trunk** = spinal cord lesion (above conus) vs **perianal/saddle loss without sensory level** = cauda equina lesion. Also: **lower motor neuron signs (areflexia, flaccid weakness)** in cauda equina vs **upper motor neuron signs (hyperreflexia, Babinski, spasticity)** in spinal cord.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Bladder scanner for post-void residual (PVR) – if unable to void or PVR >100 mL, suspect CES
  - Digital rectal exam (perineal sensation and anal tone)
  - Full neurological exam including sensory mapping of perineum (use dermatome chart)
  - Urine dipstick (exclude UTI as cause of retention)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, coagulation screen (PT/APTT), U+E, glucose, inflammatory markers (if abscess suspected – blood cultures)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Urgent MRI of the whole spine (including lumbar and thoracolumbar junction) – ideally within 1–4 hours of clinical suspicion
  - **Second-line:** CT myelogram if MRI contraindicated (e.g. non-MRI compatible pacemaker, severe claustrophobia) – but less sensitive for nerve root compression
- **Special tests:** None routine; if infection suspected – lumbar puncture (only after MRI to rule out abscess) – [discuss with senior]
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **Clinical diagnosis – no formal scoring system.** Use NICE red flags: bilateral sciatica, saddle anaesthesia, bladder retention, progressive weakness, loss of perianal sensation + reduced anal tone.
  - **Canadian C-spine rule or other** not applicable here.
- **Never-miss test:** **Urgent MRI whole spine** – omission is the single most serious error; delayed diagnosis >48 hours from symptom onset dramatically worsens prognosis for bladder/bowel recovery.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Spinal precautions** – log roll if moving patient, but no collar unless trauma; avoid flexion/rotation if vertebral instability suspected
  - **Urgent neurosurgical referral** – ideally within 1 hour of suspicion
  - **MRI as soon as possible** – do not wait for blood results
  - **Catheterisation** – if urinary retention (post-void residual >200 mL), insert a urethral catheter to decompress bladder and monitor output
  - **Analgesia** – paracetamol 1 g IV/PO, ibuprofen 400 mg PO if no contraindication (avoid NSAIDs if anticoagulated or surgical decompression imminent), add IV morphine 2.5–5 mg PRN for severe pain
  - **Do NOT give steroids** – high-dose dexamethasone is indicated only for spinal cord compression (not cauda equina); it may delay surgery and increase infection risk
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Admit under **Neurosurgery** (or Orthopaedics with neurosurgical support)
  - **Neurological observations** – hourly limb power, perianal sensation, bladder volume/catheter output
  - **Bladder chart** – strict input/output, aim for catheter removal only after neurosurgery advice
  - **Bowel care** – if faecal incontinence, consider bowel regimen (e.g. suppositories, enemas) after neurosurgery clearance
  - **VTE prophylaxis** – discuss with neurosurgery; usually low molecular weight heparin (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily) can be started **after** surgical site is haemostatic and only if no bleeding risk (e.g. spinal haematoma). In acute CES, defer until after surgical plan is clear.
  - **Surgery** – decompressive laminectomy and discectomy (or abscess drainage) ideally within 24–48 hours of symptom onset; earlier is better for neurological recovery
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Spinal rehabilitation** – physiotherapy, occupational therapy, pelvic floor physiotherapy
  - **Bladder training** – if neurogenic bladder: intermittent self-catheterisation, urology referral
  - **Bowel programme** – stimulant laxatives (senna) + suppositories, dietary fibre, bowel retraining
  - **Chronic pain management** – neuropathic agents (gabapentin/pregabalin – start low, e.g. gabapentin 300 mg nocte, titrate up) – only after acute phase
  - **Sexual health counselling** – if relevant
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride at 125 mL/h if NBM; otherwise oral free fluids
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** [check local policy] – usually enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily, but **hold** until neurosurgery confirms no spinal haematoma risk (often post-op day 1 if haemostasis adequate)
  - **Diet:** NBM if surgery planned <6 hours; otherwise normal diet
  - **Observation frequency:** Neuro obs every 4 hours (limb power, perianal sensation, bladder status)

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Analgesia:**
    - Paracetamol: 1 g PO/IV QDS (max 4 g/day)
    - Ibuprofen: 400 mg PO TDS (if no contraindication: GFR >30, no bleeding risk, not anticoagulated, not post-surgery)
    - Morphine: 5–10 mg IM/IV PRN severe pain (min 4-hourly; monitor respiratory rate)
  - **Laxatives (if chronic constipation):** Senna 15 mg PO nocte + lactulose 15 mL PO BD (after acute phase)
  - **Neuropathic pain (if chronic):** Gabapentin 300 mg PO nocte for 1 day, then 300 mg BD, then 300 mg TDS – max 3600 mg/day; or Pregabalin 75 mg PO BD, max 600 mg/day – [check local policy for titration]
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Amitriptyline 10–25 mg PO nocte for neuropathic pain (if no cardiac history, caution in elderly)
  - Oxycodone if morphine not tolerated: start 5 mg PO PRN
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid in renal impairment (GFR <30), GI bleeding, coagulopathy, and in immediate pre‑/post-operative spinal surgery (increased bleeding risk)
  - Gabapentin/pregabalin: reduce dose in renal impairment (GFR <60) – [check local policy]
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Gabapentin: reduce dose if CrCl <60 mL/min (e.g. 300 mg every other day if GFR 30–60); pregabalin: reduce similarly
  - NSAIDs: avoid if eGFR <30
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **None specific to CES** – but **withholding anticoagulation in a patient with epidural haematoma could be catastrophic**, while giving enoxaparin pre-operatively can cause spinal haemorrhage. Always verify timing with neurosurgery.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Permanent paraplegia (rare, usually due to delayed treatment or alternative diagnosis like cord infarction)
  - Urinary retention → renal injury if not catheterised
  - Sepsis if secondary UTI
  - Spinal epidural haematoma post-surgery (if anticoagulated prematurely)
- **Chronic:**
  - Irreversible bladder dysfunction (neurogenic bladder, faecal incontinence) – occurs in ~20–30% even with timely surgery
  - Chronic neuropathic pain (saddle area, perineum, legs)
  - Sexual dysfunction (erectile dysfunction, loss of vaginal sensation)
  - Lower limb weakness (persistent foot drop) – may require ankle-foot orthosis
  - Psychological distress (depression, adjustment disorder)

### Prognosis
- **Mortality:** Low (<1% from CES itself unless from underlying cause like abscess/sepsis).
- **Recovery:** Best if surgical decompression within 48 hours of symptom onset – up to 80% recover bladder function; if >48 hours, permanent bowel/bladder dysfunction is common. Motor recovery (leg strength) can occur over weeks to months but may be incomplete.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **I**n any patient with acute back pain + leg symptoms, **always ask about bladder function and perianal sensation** – this non-intrusive question can catch CES early.
- **Perform a bladder scan** (post-void residual) at the bedside – a PVR >100 mL (or >200 mL in some guidelines) is the single most objective finding and often prompts timely MRI.
- **If you suspect CES, call neurosurgery immediately** – do not wait for MRI; they can organise and prioritise imaging while you prepare the patient.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT** send a patient with bilateral sciatica to physiotherapy or prescribe simple analgesia and discharge without assessing perianal sensation and bladder function.
- **Do NOT** rely solely on a normal digital rectal exam (DRE) – early CES can have normal anal tone; absence of perianal sensation is more sensitive.
- **Do NOT** delay MRI in favour of a CT spine (lumbar) – CT misses disc fragments and early nerve root compression; MRI is mandatory.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** (rarely, if MRI negative and symptoms mild)
  - Any new or worsening leg weakness (e.g. foot drop, inability to stand from chair)
  - Loss of bladder control (inability to pass urine or leaking)
  - New numbness or tingling in the saddle area (between legs, around anus)
  - Severe or progressive back pain not controlled by simple analgesia
- **Follow-up:** **Neurosurgery or Orthopaedic spine clinic** within 2–4 weeks; **community physiotherapy**; **GP review** in 1 week for medication and neurological status.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / neurosurgery registrar immediately if:**
  - Sudden deterioration in limb power (e.g. dropping from MRC 4 to 2 over 1 hour)
  - New onset of urinary retention (inability to void, PVR >200 mL)
  - Loss of perianal sensation previously present
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 (unlikely from CES alone but consider sepsis from UTI/dehydration) – treat as medical emergency
  - Lactate > 2 mmol/L (if measured – may indicate ischaemia or sepsis)
- **Call crash team / ICU if:**
  - GCS < 13 (rare but consider meningitis/spinal shock) or cardiorespiratory compromise from underlying cause (e.g. aortic dissection, myelopathy)
